messages.summary: Build Phase 1 of the complete Idea-to-Project pipeline: Reception and Deep Analysis.
messages.originalContent:
Build Phase 1 of the complete Idea-to-Project pipeline: Reception and Deep Analysis.

Project: /root/genplatform. Site: https://app.gen3.ai.
DESIGN PROTECTION: Do NOT modify sidebar.tsx, navbar.tsx, layout.tsx, globals.css.

Task 1: Enhance the Ideas Lab page. When user submits a new idea, show an immediate pipeline tracker:
- Horizontal progress bar with 7 stages as circles connected by lines:
  ● Submitted → ● Analyzing → ● Review → ● Planning → ● Task Generation → ● Development → ● Complete
- Current stage is highlighted (amber pulsing), completed stages are green, future stages are gray
- This tracker appears at the top of the idea detail view

Task 2: Create src/app/api/ideas/[id]/deep-analyze/route.ts — comprehensive analysis:
- POST sends to OpenClaw via Telegram with this detailed prompt:
"🔬 DEEP ANALYSIS — Idea: {title}
Description: {description}

Provide a comprehensive professional analysis in JSON format:
{
  "summary": "2-3 sentence overview",
  "marketAnalysis": {
    "marketSize": "estimated TAM",
    "growthRate": "annual growth %",
    "competitors": [{"name":"","strength":"","weakness":""}],
    "opportunity": "what gap this fills"
  },
  "technicalAnalysis": {
    "feasibility": 1-10,
    "recommendedStack": ["Next.js","PostgreSQL",...],
    "aiTools": ["which AI/ML tools to use"],
    "hostingNeeds": "server requirements",
    "estimatedDevTime": "X weeks"
  },
  "features": {
    "mvp": ["feature1","feature2"],
    "phase2": ["feature3","feature4"],
    "future": ["feature5","feature6"]
  },
  "costEstimate": {
    "development": "$X",
    "hosting": "$X/month",
    "apis": "$X/month",
    "total": "$X"
  },
  "risks": ["risk1","risk2"],
  "recommendation": "GO or NO-GO with reasoning"
}"
- Returns {success: true, analysisId}

Task 3: When analysis response comes back from OpenClaw, parse and display in the idea detail:
- Market Analysis card: competitors table, market size, opportunity
- Technical card: feasibility score (visual gauge), tech stack badges, dev time
- Features card: MVP features list, Phase 2 list, Future list
- Cost card: breakdown table
- Recommendation card: GO/NO-GO with big badge

Task 4: Below the analysis, show two buttons:
- "✅ Approve Analysis — Proceed to Planning" → moves to next stage
- "✏️ Request Revision — Modify and Re-analyze" → lets user add notes and re-triggers analysis
- "❌ Reject — Archive Idea" → moves idea to rejected

Task 5: Store analysis results in the idea object via PUT /api/ideas/[id] with analysisData field.

After ALL: npm run build && pm2 restart genplatform-app && git add -A && git commit -m "Idea pipeline: deep analysis with structured results" && git push

🔗 Preview: https://app.gen3.ai/ideas
Do all 5 tasks NOW.

messages.summary: Build Phase 2 of pipeline: Improvement Review and Architecture Planning.
messages.originalContent:
Build Phase 2 of pipeline: Improvement Review and Architecture Planning.

Project: /root/genplatform. Site: https://app.gen3.ai.
DESIGN PROTECTION: Do NOT modify sidebar.tsx, navbar.tsx, layout.tsx, globals.css.

Task 1: Create src/app/api/ideas/[id]/improve/route.ts:
- POST sends to OpenClaw: "📈 IMPROVEMENT REVIEW for: {title}
  Current analysis: {summary of analysis}
  Current features: {MVP features list}
  
  Review this idea and suggest:
  1. Additional features that would increase value
  2. Better technical approaches
  3. UX improvements
  4. Monetization strategies not yet considered
  5. Integration opportunities (APIs, platforms, partnerships)
  6. Competitive advantages to develop
  
  Return as JSON: {improvements: [{title, description, impact: high/medium/low, effort: high/medium/low}], overallScore: 1-10}"

Task 2: Display improvements in idea detail as a card list:
- Each improvement: title, description, impact badge (green/amber/red), effort badge
- Checkbox to accept/reject each improvement
- "Apply Selected Improvements" button → updates the idea features and re-saves

Task 3: Create src/app/api/ideas/[id]/architect/route.ts:
- POST sends to OpenClaw: "📋 ARCHITECTURE PLAN for: {title}
  Approved features: {list}
  Tech stack: {stack}
  
  Create a detailed architecture plan:
  1. System architecture (components, services, databases)
  2. File structure (folders, key files)
  3. API endpoints needed
  4. Database schema (tables, relationships)
  5. Third-party integrations
  6. Deployment strategy
  
  Return as JSON with clear structure"

Task 4: Display architecture plan as:
- System diagram (text-based, showing components and connections)
- File structure tree
- API endpoints table
- Database tables list
- "Approve Architecture — Generate Tasks" button

Task 5: Pipeline tracker at top updates: Analysis ✅ → Improvement ✅ → Architecture (current) → ...

After ALL: npm run build && pm2 restart genplatform-app && git add -A && git commit -m "Idea pipeline: improvement review + architecture planning" && git push

🔗 Preview: https://app.gen3.ai/ideas
Do all 5 tasks NOW.

tasks.originalDescription: Create src/app/api/ideas/[id]/improve/route.ts:

microTasks.change: Create POST route handler with Prisma query
microTasks.description: Create a new API route file at this exact path. Import NextRequest and NextResponse from 'next/server'. Import the prisma client from '@/lib/prisma'. Create and export an async POST function that takes request: NextRequest and a params object containing the dynamic id parameter. Extract the id from params using destructuring. At the start of the function, query prisma.idea.findUnique with where: { id } to fetch the existing idea record including all its fields like title, analysis, mvpFeatures, techStack.

microTasks.change: Build the OpenClaw prompt string
microTasks.description: After fetching the idea, check if it exists - if not, return NextResponse.json({ error: 'Idea not found' }, { status: 404 }). Then construct the prompt string using template literals: start with '📈 IMPROVEMENT REVIEW for: ' followed by idea.title. Add 'Current analysis: ' with idea.analysis or a summary. Add 'Current features: ' with idea.mvpFeatures joined as a comma-separated list. Include the full instruction block asking for: 1. Additional features that would increase value, 2. Better technical approaches, 3. UX improvements, 4. Monetization strategies not yet considered, 5. Integration opportunities, 6. Competitive advantages to develop. End with 'Return as JSON: {improvements: [{title, description, impact: high/medium/low, effort: high/medium/low}], overallScore: 1-10}'

microTasks.change: Send prompt to OpenClaw and parse response
microTasks.description: Make a fetch POST request to the OpenClaw API endpoint (use process.env.OPENCLAW_API_URL or the direct URL). Set headers with Content-Type: application/json and Authorization: Bearer with process.env.OPENCLAW_API_KEY. The body should be JSON.stringify({ prompt, model: 'gpt-4' }). Await the response and parse it as JSON. Extract the improvements array and overallScore from the OpenClaw response. Handle any errors with try-catch and return appropriate error responses with status 500.

microTasks.change: Save improvements to database and return response
microTasks.description: After receiving the improvements from OpenClaw, use prisma.idea.update to save the improvements to the database. The update should use where: { id } and data: { improvements: JSON.stringify(improvements), improvementScore: overallScore, status: 'improvement_review' }. Return NextResponse.json({ success: true, improvements, overallScore }) on successful completion. Make sure the improvements field in your Prisma schema can store JSON - if it's a String type, stringify the array before saving.

microTasks.description: At the top of the IdeaDetailPage component or wherever the idea detail is rendered, add a new state variable using useState: const [improvements, setImprovements] = useState<Array<{title: string, description: string, impact: string, effort: string, selected: boolean}>>([]). Also add const [loadingImprovements, setLoadingImprovements] = useState(false). In the useEffect that fetches the idea data, after loading the idea, check if idea.improvements exists and parse it with JSON.parse, then map over it to add a selected: false property to each item before calling setImprovements.

microTasks.change: Add function to fetch improvements from API
microTasks.description: Create a new function called handleGetImprovements that is async. Set setLoadingImprovements(true). Make a POST fetch to `/api/ideas/${id}/improve`. Parse the JSON response. Map over response.improvements to add selected: false to each, then call setImprovements with the result. Set setLoadingImprovements(false) in a finally block. Add error handling with try-catch that shows a toast.error('Failed to get improvements'). Create a button in the UI that calls this function: <Button onClick={handleGetImprovements} disabled={loadingImprovements}>Get Improvement Suggestions</Button>.

microTasks.change: Render improvements as a card list with checkboxes
microTasks.description: Below the existing idea details section, add a new section conditionally rendered when improvements.length > 0. Create a Card component wrapper with a CardHeader containing title 'Improvement Suggestions' and the overall score as a badge. Inside CardContent, map over the improvements array. For each improvement, render a div with: a Checkbox component, the improvement.title in bold, improvement.description as paragraph text. The Checkbox should have checked={improvement.selected} and onChange should call a function that toggles the selected state for that specific improvement by index.

microTasks.change: Add impact and effort badges with color coding
microTasks.description: For each improvement card item, add two Badge components next to the title. The first badge shows impact: use variant='default' with className='bg-green-500' for 'high', 'bg-amber-500' for 'medium', 'bg-red-500' for 'low'. The second badge shows effort with the same color logic but inverted (high effort = red, low effort = green). Use a helper function getImpactColor(impact: string) and getEffortColor(effort: string) that return the appropriate Tailwind classes. Display the badge text as 'Impact: {impact}' and 'Effort: {effort}'.

microTasks.change: Add selection toggle and apply improvements functionality
microTasks.description: Add a toggleImprovement function: const toggleImprovement = (index: number) => { setImprovements(prev => prev.map((imp, i) => i === index ? {...imp, selected: !imp.selected} : imp)); }. Below the improvements list, add a Button labeled 'Apply Selected Improvements'. Create an async function handleApplyImprovements that filters improvements where selected === true, then makes a PATCH request to `/api/ideas/${id}` with body containing the selected improvements to merge into mvpFeatures. After success, show toast.success('Improvements applied!') and refresh the idea data.

microTasks.change: Create architect route with Prisma query

Export an async POST function with request: NextRequest and params containing the id. Query the idea using prisma.idea.findUnique({ where: { id }, select: { id: true, title: true, mvpFeatures: true, techStack: true, improvements: true } }). Return 404 error if idea not found. microTasks.change: Call OpenClaw API and parse architecture response
microTasks.description: Send the prompt to OpenClaw API using fetch POST to the OpenClaw endpoint. Include headers: Content-Type application/json, Authorization Bearer token from process.env.OPENCLAW_API_KEY. Body should contain { prompt, model: 'gpt-4', responseFormat: 'json' }. Wrap in try-catch for error handling. Parse the response JSON and extract the architecture object containing: systemArchitecture, fileStructure, apiEndpoints, databaseSchema, integrations, deploymentStrategy.

microTasks.change: Persist architecture to database and return response
microTasks.description: Save the architecture plan to the database using prisma.idea.update with where: { id } and data: { architecture: JSON.stringify(architectureData), status: 'architecture_complete' }. If the architecture field doesn't exist in your schema, you may need to add it as a String type. Return NextResponse.json({ success: true, architecture: architectureData }). Include the full architecture object in the response so the frontend can display it immediately without another fetch.

Add const [loadingArchitecture, setLoadingArchitecture] = useState(false). In the useEffect that loads idea data, if idea.architecture exists, parse it and call setArchitecture. microTasks.change: Render API endpoints as a styled table
microTasks.description: Create an APIEndpointsTable section. Render a Card with title 'API Endpoints'. Inside, create a table using either HTML table tags or a Table component from your UI library. The table should have columns: Method (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE with colored badges), Path (/api/...), Description, Auth Required (yes/no badge). For each table, show the table name as a heading, then a small table of columns showing name, type (String, Int, etc), and constraints (PK, FK, unique). microTasks.change: Add approve and generate tasks button

Style it with className='w-full mt-4 bg-gradient-to-r from-green-500 to-emerald-600'. Create an async function handleApproveArchitecture that makes a POST to `/api/ideas/${id}/tasks/generate` (to be created in next phase), then on success shows toast.success('Tasks generated!') and navigates to a tasks view or updates the pipeline status. tasks.originalDescription: Pipeline tracker at top updates: Analysis ✅ → Improvement ✅ → Architecture (current) → ...

microTasks.change: Define pipeline stages array and status mapping
microTasks.description: At the top of the idea detail page content (after the page title but before the main content), add a PipelineTracker component. Create a const PIPELINE_STAGES = [{id: 'analysis', label: 'Analysis'}, {id: 'improvement', label: 'Improvement'}, {id: 'architecture', label: 'Architecture'}, {id: 'tasks', label: 'Tasks'}, {id: 'development', label: 'Development'}]. Determine the current stage based on idea.status field - map status values like 'analyzed', 'improvement_review', 'architecture_complete' to the corresponding stage id.

microTasks.change: Render pipeline stages with status indicators
microTasks.description: Render the pipeline tracker as a horizontal flex container with items-center and justify-between. Map over PIPELINE_STAGES. For each stage, render a div containing: a circular indicator (w-8 h-8 rounded-full flex items-center justify-center), the stage label below it. Determine if stage is complete (before current), current, or pending. Complete stages: bg-green-500 with a checkmark ✅ icon. Current stage: bg-blue-500 with a pulsing ring animation (animate-ping). Pending stages: bg-gray-300 with empty circle.

microTasks.change: Add connecting lines between pipeline stages
microTasks.description: Between each stage indicator, render a connecting line. Use a div with className='flex-1 h-1 mx-2'. If the stage before this line is complete, make it bg-green-500. If it's the current stage, use a gradient from green to gray: bg-gradient-to-r from-green-500 to-gray-300. If both surrounding stages are pending, use bg-gray-300. This creates a visual progress bar effect showing how far through the pipeline the idea has progressed. Ensure the first stage has no line before it and the last has no line after.

microTasks.change: Add interactivity and navigation to pipeline tracker
microTasks.description: Make the pipeline tracker interactive. When a completed stage is clicked, scroll to that section of the page using document.getElementById and scrollIntoView({ behavior: 'smooth' }). Add IDs to each section: id='section-analysis', id='section-improvement', id='section-architecture'. Add cursor-pointer and hover:scale-105 transition to completed stages. For the current stage, add a tooltip or small text below saying '(Current Step)'. Pending stages should have cursor-not-allowed and opacity-50 to indicate they can't be accessed yet.

messages.summary: Build Phase 3: Task Generation and Agent Distribution.
messages.originalContent:
Build Phase 3: Task Generation and Agent Distribution.

Project: /root/genplatform. Site: https://app.gen3.ai.
DESIGN PROTECTION: Do NOT modify sidebar.tsx, navbar.tsx, layout.tsx, globals.css.

Task 1: Create src/app/api/ideas/[id]/generate-tasks/route.ts:
- POST sends to OpenClaw: "📋 TASK GENERATION for project: {title}
  Architecture: {architecture summary}
  Features: {features list}
  
  Generate ALL tasks needed to build this project. Be extremely thorough — generate as many tasks as needed for professional quality. For EACH task provide:
  {
    name: 'task name',
    description: 'detailed description of what to do',
    department: 'Frontend/Backend/Security/QA/Research/Planning',
    estimatedMinutes: number,
    priority: 'high/medium/low',
    phase: 'MVP/Phase2/Phase3',
    dependencies: ['task names this depends on']
  }
  
  Generate minimum 50 tasks for a thorough project. Cover: setup, frontend pages, API routes, database, auth, testing, security, deployment, documentation."

Task 2: When tasks come back, create them all via POST /api/tasks (loop):
- Each task gets: projectId from the new project, status='backlog', sprint assignment based on phase
- Show progress: "Creating task 1/73... 15/73... 73/73 ✅"
- All tasks appear in the Kanban board

Task 3: Auto-assign tasks to departments using src/lib/task-router.ts:
- Frontend keywords → 💻 Frontend Dev
- API/database keywords → ⚙️ Backend Dev
- Test/review keywords → 🔍 QA
- Security keywords → 🛡️ Security
- Show assignment summary: "Frontend: 25 tasks | Backend: 20 | QA: 15 | Security: 8 | Other: 5"

Task 4: Create the project automatically:
- POST /api/projects with idea's data (name, description, tech stack from analysis)
- Set previewUrl to "{slug}.gen3.ai"
- Link all generated tasks to this project
- Navigate to the new project detail page

Task 5: Show the task distribution as a visual:
- Pie chart or horizontal bar showing tasks per department
- Priority breakdown: high/medium/low counts
- Phase breakdown: MVP/Phase2/Phase3 counts
- "Start Development" button → changes first batch of tasks to "planned"

After ALL: npm run build && pm2 restart genplatform-app && git add -A && git commit -m "Idea pipeline: task generation + auto-assignment" && git push

🔗 Preview: https://app.gen3.ai/ideas
Do all 5 tasks NOW.

tasks.originalDescription: Create src/app/api/ideas/[id]/generate-tasks/route.ts:

microTasks.change: Create basic API route structure with imports and error handling
microTasks.description: Create the new API route file with the basic Next.js App Router structure. Import NextRequest and NextResponse from 'next/server'. Import prisma from '@/lib/prisma'. Export an async function POST that accepts (request: NextRequest, { params }: { params: { id: string } }). Extract the idea ID from params.id. Add a try-catch block that returns NextResponse.json with appropriate status codes (200 for success, 404 for not found, 500 for errors).

microTasks.change: Add database query to fetch idea with analysis data
microTasks.description: Inside the POST function's try block, fetch the idea from the database using prisma.idea.findUnique({ where: { id: params.id }, include: { analysis: true } }). If the idea is not found, return NextResponse.json({ error: 'Idea not found' }, { status: 404 }). Store the result in a const called 'idea'. Extract the architecture and features from idea.analysis if it exists, otherwise use empty defaults.

microTasks.change: Build the comprehensive task generation prompt string
Add OpenClaw API integration for task generation
microTasks.description: Make the API call to OpenClaw using fetch. Create const openClawResponse = await fetch(process.env.OPENCLAW_API_URL + '/chat', { method: 'POST', headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json', 'Authorization': 'Bearer ' + process.env.OPENCLAW_API_KEY }, body: JSON.stringify({ message: taskGenerationPrompt, model: 'gpt-4' }) }). Parse the response with const clawData = await openClawResponse.json(). Extract the tasks array from clawData.response, parsing JSON if needed using JSON.parse().

microTasks.change: Add task validation and default value assignment
microTasks.description: Add response validation and transformation. Create a function validateTask(task: any): boolean that checks if task has required fields (name, description, department). Filter the parsed tasks array using this validator. Map each valid task to include default values: estimatedMinutes: task.estimatedMinutes || 30, priority: task.priority || 'medium', phase: task.phase || 'MVP', dependencies: task.dependencies || []. Return NextResponse.json({ tasks: validatedTasks, count: validatedTasks.length, ideaId: params.id }).

tasks.originalDescription: When tasks come back, create them all via POST /api/tasks (loop):

microTasks.change: Add loop structure for bulk task creation with progress tracking
microTasks.description: After validating tasks, add the bulk creation logic. Create an array const createdTasks = []. Use a for loop: for (let i = 0; i < validatedTasks.length; i++) to iterate through each task. Inside the loop, prepare the task data object with all required fields mapping from the OpenClaw response to your database schema. Include a progress tracking mechanism by adding progressIndex: i + 1 and totalTasks: validatedTasks.length to track creation progress.

microTasks.change: Implement individual task creation with database persistence
microTasks.description: Inside the loop, create each task using prisma.task.create({ data: { title: task.name, description: task.description, status: 'backlog', priority: task.priority, department: task.department, estimatedMinutes: task.estimatedMinutes, phase: task.phase, dependencies: task.dependencies, projectId: null, ideaId: params.id, sprintId: await getSprintIdByPhase(task.phase) } }). Push the created task to createdTasks array. Handle individual task creation errors with try-catch to continue on failure.

microTasks.change: Add sprint assignment helper function based on task phase
microTasks.description: Create a helper async function getSprintIdByPhase(phase: string): Promise<string | null> before the POST function. This function should query prisma.sprint.findFirst({ where: { name: { contains: phase } } }) and return sprint?.id || null. This maps task phases (MVP, Phase2, Phase3) to existing sprints in the database. If no matching sprint exists, the task will have null sprintId which is acceptable for backlog items.

microTasks.change: Enhance response with comprehensive progress data
microTasks.description: Modify the final response to include detailed progress information. Return NextResponse.json({ success: true, message: `Created ${createdTasks.length}/${validatedTasks.length} tasks`, tasks: createdTasks, progress: { total: validatedTasks.length, created: createdTasks.length, failed: validatedTasks.length - createdTasks.length }, ideaId: params.id }). This provides the frontend with all necessary data to show the '1/73... 15/73... 73/73 ✅' progress updates.

microTasks.change: Create progress component with TypeScript interfaces and state
microTasks.description: Create a new component file for the task generation progress display. Import React, useState, useEffect from 'react'. Import Progress from '@/components/ui/progress'. Import CheckCircle, Loader2 from 'lucide-react'. Create interface TaskGenerationProgressProps { ideaId: string; onComplete: (tasks: any[]) => void; }. Export default function TaskGenerationProgress({ ideaId, onComplete }: TaskGenerationProgressProps) with state for progress (number), total (number), current (number), and status ('generating' | 'creating' | 'complete').

microTasks.change: Implement API call with real-time progress updates and completion callback
microTasks.description: Inside TaskGenerationProgress, add useEffect that calls the generate-tasks API. Use fetch(`/api/ideas/${ideaId}/generate-tasks`, { method: 'POST' }). On response, update states: setTotal(data.progress.total), setCurrent(data.progress.created), setProgress((data.progress.created / data.progress.total) * 100). When complete (progress === 100), setStatus('complete') and call onComplete(data.tasks). Render a Card with Progress bar showing '{current}/{total}' and status text like 'Creating task 15/73...' or '73/73 ✅'.

tasks.originalDescription: Auto-assign tasks to departments using src/lib/task-router.ts:

microTasks.change: Create task router file with department constants and types
microTasks.description: Create the new task router utility file. Define the department types as a const DEPARTMENTS = { FRONTEND: '💻 Frontend Dev', BACKEND: '⚙️ Backend Dev', QA: '🔍 QA', SECURITY: '🛡️ Security', RESEARCH: '📚 Research', PLANNING: '📋 Planning' } as const. Create type Department = typeof DEPARTMENTS[keyof typeof DEPARTMENTS]. Export these types for use in other files.

microTasks.change: Add comprehensive keyword mappings for each department
microTasks.description: Define keyword mappings as const DEPARTMENT_KEYWORDS: Record<string, string[]>. For FRONTEND: ['react', 'component', 'ui', 'css', 'tailwind', 'page', 'layout', 'form', 'button', 'modal', 'responsive', 'animation', 'style']. For BACKEND: ['api', 'database', 'prisma', 'route', 'endpoint', 'server', 'migration', 'schema', 'query', 'crud']. For QA: ['test', 'testing', 'review', 'qa', 'quality', 'bug', 'fix', 'validate', 'check']. For SECURITY: ['security', 'auth', 'authentication', 'authorization', 'encrypt', 'token', 'permission', 'rbac', 'sanitize'].

microTasks.change: Implement keyword-based department routing algorithm
microTasks.description: Create the main routing function: export function routeTaskToDepartment(task: { name: string; description: string; department?: string }): string. First check if task already has a valid department assignment and return it if so. Combine task.name and task.description into lowercase searchText. Loop through DEPARTMENT_KEYWORDS entries, counting keyword matches for each department. Return the department with the highest match count, defaulting to DEPARTMENTS.PLANNING if no matches found.

microTasks.change: Create bulk routing function with summary statistics
microTasks.description: Add a bulk routing function: export function routeAllTasks(tasks: Array<{ name: string; description: string; department?: string }>): { tasks: Array<{ ...task; assignedDepartment: string }>; summary: Record<string, number> }. Map through all tasks calling routeTaskToDepartment for each. Build a summary object counting tasks per department. Return both the enriched tasks array and the summary object for display purposes.

microTasks.change: Add summary formatting utility for display
microTasks.description: Export a summary formatter function: export function formatDepartmentSummary(summary: Record<string, number>): string. Map through the summary entries to create the display string format: 'Frontend: 25 tasks | Backend: 20 | QA: 15 | Security: 8 | Other: 5'. Use Object.entries(summary).map(([dept, count]) => `${dept}: ${count}`).join(' | '). This provides the exact format requested for the assignment summary display.

microTasks.change: Integrate task router into the generate-tasks API endpoint
microTasks.description: Import the task router at the top of the file: import { routeAllTasks, formatDepartmentSummary } from '@/lib/task-router'. After validating tasks but before creating them in the database, call const { tasks: routedTasks, summary } = routeAllTasks(validatedTasks). Use routedTasks instead of validatedTasks in the creation loop. Include the summary and formatted summary string in the API response for the frontend to display.

tasks.originalDescription: Create the project automatically:

microTasks.change: Create helper function for project creation with slug generation
microTasks.description: After task generation but before bulk task creation, add project creation logic. Create a helper function createProjectFromIdea(idea: any): Promise<Project>. Inside, generate a slug using: const slug = idea.title.toLowerCase().replace(/[^a-z0-9]+/g, '-').replace(/(^-|-$)/g, ''). Set previewUrl as `${slug}.gen3.ai`. Extract techStack from idea.analysis?.technologies || ['Next.js', 'TypeScript', 'Tailwind'].

microTasks.change: Implement database project creation with all required fields
microTasks.description: Inside createProjectFromIdea function, use prisma.project.create({ data: { name: idea.title, description: idea.description, status: 'planning', previewUrl: `${slug}.gen3.ai`, techStack: techStack, ideaId: idea.id, createdAt: new Date(), settings: { autoAssign: true, notifications: true } } }). Return the created project. Handle potential slug conflicts by appending a random suffix if prisma throws a unique constraint error.

microTasks.change: Link tasks to project and update idea status
microTasks.description: Modify the task creation loop to use the newly created project. Before the loop, call const project = await createProjectFromIdea(idea). Inside the loop, change projectId: null to projectId: project.id in the prisma.task.create call. This links all generated tasks to the new project. Update the idea status to 'converted' using prisma.idea.update({ where: { id: params.id }, data: { status: 'converted', projectId: project.id } }).

microTasks.change: Add project data and redirect URL to API response
microTasks.description: Enhance the final API response to include project data for navigation. Modify the return statement to include: project: { id: project.id, name: project.name, slug: slug, previewUrl: project.previewUrl }, redirectUrl: `/projects/${project.id}`. This allows the frontend to immediately navigate to the new project's detail page after generation completes. Add project creation timestamp for tracking.

microTasks.change: Implement automatic navigation to new project page
microTasks.description: Add navigation handling after completion. Import { useRouter } from 'next/navigation' at the top. Inside the component, add const router = useRouter(). Modify the onComplete callback in useEffect to also trigger navigation: if (data.redirectUrl) { setTimeout(() => router.push(data.redirectUrl), 1500); }. Add a state for showingRedirect and display 'Redirecting to project...' message after tasks complete. Show the project preview URL in the completion message.

tasks.originalDescription: Show the task distribution as a visual:

microTasks.change: Create distribution chart component with required imports and types
microTasks.description: Create a new component file for the distribution visualization. Import React from 'react'. Import { Card, CardHeader, CardTitle, CardContent } from '@/components/ui/card'. Import { PieChart, Pie, Cell, ResponsiveContainer, Legend, Tooltip } from 'recharts'. Define interface TaskDistributionProps { departmentSummary: Record<string, number>; prioritySummary: Record<string, number>; phaseSummary: Record<string, number>; onStartDevelopment: () => void; }.

microTasks.change: Add color constants for all chart categories
microTasks.description: Define color constants for the charts: const DEPARTMENT_COLORS = { '💻 Frontend Dev': '#3b82f6', '⚙️ Backend Dev': '#10b981', '🔍 QA': '#f59e0b', '🛡️ Security': '#ef4444', '📚 Research': '#8b5cf6', '📋 Planning': '#6b7280' }. const PRIORITY_COLORS = { high: '#ef4444', medium: '#f59e0b', low: '#10b981' }. const PHASE_COLORS = { MVP: '#3b82f6', Phase2: '#8b5cf6', Phase3: '#10b981' }. These provide consistent visual styling.

microTasks.change: Transform summary data into recharts-compatible format
microTasks.description: Inside the component function, transform the summary objects into chart data arrays. Create const departmentData = Object.entries(departmentSummary).map(([name, value]) => ({ name, value, color: DEPARTMENT_COLORS[name] || '#6b7280' })). Do the same for priorityData and phaseData using their respective color mappings. Calculate totalTasks = Object.values(departmentSummary).reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0) for display in the header.

microTasks.change: Create department distribution pie chart card
microTasks.description: Build the render JSX with three cards in a grid. Use className='grid grid-cols-1 md:grid-cols-3 gap-4'. First Card: Department Distribution with PieChart showing departmentData, innerRadius={60}, outerRadius={80}, paddingAngle={5}. Map through data to create Cell components with fill={entry.color}. Add Legend and Tooltip. Include total count badge showing '{totalTasks} total tasks'.

microTasks.change: Create priority breakdown visualization with horizontal bars
microTasks.description: Add second Card for Priority Breakdown. Use a horizontal bar chart approach with div elements instead of PieChart for variety. Create div with className='space-y-3'. Map through priorityData: for each priority, render a label span, a progress bar div with width={`${(value/totalTasks)*100}%`} and background color from PRIORITY_COLORS, and a count span. Show 'high: 15 | medium: 30 | low: 28' style breakdown below the bars.

microTasks.change: Create phase breakdown visualization with time estimate
microTasks.description: Add third Card for Phase Breakdown using similar horizontal bar approach as priority. Map through phaseData showing MVP, Phase2, Phase3 with their respective counts and progress bars. Use PHASE_COLORS for styling. Add explanatory text below: 'MVP tasks will be prioritized first'. Include the total estimated time calculation: const totalMinutes = tasks.reduce((a, t) => a + t.estimatedMinutes, 0); display as '{Math.round(totalMinutes/60)} hours estimated'.

microTasks.change: Add Start Development button with styling and callback
microTasks.description: Add the 'Start Development' button section below the three cards. Import { Button } from '@/components/ui/button'. Import { Play } from 'lucide-react'. Create a div with className='mt-6 flex justify-center'. Render Button with onClick={onStartDevelopment}, className='bg-green-600 hover:bg-green-700 text-white px-8 py-3 text-lg'. Include Play icon and text 'Start Development'. Add helper text below: 'This will move MVP tasks to planned status'.

microTasks.change: Create API endpoint to transition MVP tasks to planned status
microTasks.description: Create a new API route for starting development. Import NextRequest, NextResponse from 'next/server' and prisma from '@/lib/prisma'. Export async function POST that updates tasks: await prisma.task.updateMany({ where: { projectId: params.id, phase: 'MVP', status: 'backlog' }, data: { status: 'planned' } }). Return the count of updated tasks. This powers the 'Start Development' button functionality.

messages.summary: Build Phase 4: Parallel Execution and Live Monitoring.
messages.originalContent:
Build Phase 4: Parallel Execution and Live Monitoring.

Project: /root/genplatform. Site: https://app.gen3.ai.
DESIGN PROTECTION: Do NOT modify sidebar.tsx, navbar.tsx, layout.tsx, globals.css.

Task 1: Create src/app/api/projects/[id]/execute/route.ts:
- POST starts execution of planned tasks
- Takes the first batch of "planned" tasks (e.g. 5 at a time)
- For each task: sends to OpenClaw via Telegram with the format:
  "🎯 EXECUTE TASK: [{department}] {taskName}
  Project: {projectName} at {projectPath}
  Description: {description}
  Dependencies: {list or 'none'}
  
  Complete this task. Follow all WORK-RULES. Send notification when done."
- Marks tasks as "in_progress"
- Returns {startedTasks: N, totalPlanned: M}

Task 2: In the project detail page, add "Execution Control" panel:
- "▶️ Start Batch" button → executes next 5 planned tasks
- "⏸️ Pause" button → stops sending new tasks (current ones finish)
- "⏭️ Auto Mode" toggle → automatically starts next batch when current finishes
- Batch size selector: 1, 3, 5, 10 tasks at a time

Task 3: Live monitoring in the project detail:
- Real-time task status panel showing:
  * Tasks in queue (planned): X
  * Currently executing: Y (with names and assigned departments)
  * Completed today: Z
  * Failed (needs attention): N
- Each executing task shows: name, department icon, elapsed time, progress indicator (spinner)

Task 4: Activity feed for the project:
- Show messages from OpenClaw about this project
- "✅ Task T-15 done — Created login page"
- "🔨 Working on T-16 — API routes..."
- "❌ T-17 failed — fixing import error"
- Auto-scroll, new items at top

Task 5: Create a "Daily Schedule" option in project settings:
- "Auto-execute from [time] to [time]" (e.g. 8 AM to 11 PM)
- Uses cron job: at start time, begins executing tasks. At end time, pauses.
- Toggle: ON/OFF
- This creates an OpenClaw cron job for the project

After ALL: npm run build && pm2 restart genplatform-app && git add -A && git commit -m "Project execution: parallel tasks, live monitoring, auto mode" && git push

🔗 Preview: https://app.gen3.ai/projects/1
Do all 5 tasks NOW.

tasks.originalDescription: Create src/app/api/projects/[id]/execute/route.ts:

microTasks.change: Create base API route structure with imports and POST handler
microTasks.description: Create a new API route file. Import NextRequest and NextResponse from 'next/server', import prisma from '@/lib/prisma'. Define and export an async POST function that takes request: NextRequest and context with params containing id: string. Extract the project ID from params. Add try-catch wrapper for all database operations. Return NextResponse.json with appropriate status codes.

microTasks.change: Add logic to fetch planned tasks from database with batch size limit
microTasks.description: Inside the POST handler, parse the request body using await request.json() to extract optional batchSize parameter (default to 5). Query the database using prisma.task.findMany with where clause: projectId equals the URL param id AND status equals 'planned'. Add orderBy to sort by priority descending and createdAt ascending. Use take: batchSize to limit results. Store results in plannedTasks variable.

microTasks.change: Fetch project details for Telegram message formatting
microTasks.description: After fetching planned tasks, query the project details using prisma.project.findUnique with where id equals the param. Include the project's name and path fields. If project is null, return NextResponse.json with error 'Project not found' and status 404. Store project in a const variable for use in message formatting.

microTasks.change: Create helper function to format task messages for Telegram
Create function to send messages to OpenClaw via Telegram API
microTasks.description: Create an async function sendToOpenClaw that takes message: string as parameter. Use fetch to POST to process.env.TELEGRAM_BOT_API or a configured endpoint. The body should be JSON.stringify with chat_id from process.env.OPENCLAW_CHAT_ID and text: message. Set headers Content-Type to application/json. Return the response.ok boolean. Wrap in try-catch, return false on error.

microTasks.change: Implement parallel task execution with status updates
microTasks.description: In the POST handler after fetching tasks, use Promise.all to iterate over plannedTasks. For each task: call formatTaskForTelegram(task, project), then await sendToOpenClaw with the formatted message, then update the task status using prisma.task.update with where id: task.id and data status: 'in_progress' plus startedAt: new Date(). Collect results in startedTasks array.

microTasks.change: Return execution summary with counts and task IDs
microTasks.description: After processing all tasks, count remaining planned tasks using prisma.task.count with where projectId and status 'planned'. Return NextResponse.json containing: startedTasks as the length of successfully started tasks array, totalPlanned as the count of remaining planned tasks, taskIds as array of started task IDs. Add appropriate error handling for the entire operation.

tasks.originalDescription: In the project detail page, add "Execution Control" panel:

microTasks.change: Add imports for execution control UI components and icons
microTasks.description: At the top of the file after existing imports, add: import { Play, Pause, SkipForward, Settings2 } from 'lucide-react'. Also import useState if not already imported. Import Select, SelectContent, SelectItem, SelectTrigger, SelectValue from '@/components/ui/select'. Import Switch from '@/components/ui/switch' for the auto mode toggle.

microTasks.change: Add state variables for execution control
microTasks.description: Inside the main component function, add new state variables: const [isExecuting, setIsExecuting] = useState(false) for tracking execution state, const [isPaused, setIsPaused] = useState(false) for pause state, const [autoMode, setAutoMode] = useState(false) for auto execution toggle, const [batchSize, setBatchSize] = useState('5') for batch size selector defaulting to 5 tasks.

microTasks.change: Implement start batch execution handler
microTasks.description: Create an async function handleStartBatch that: sets isExecuting to true, calls fetch with POST to `/api/projects/${id}/execute` with body JSON.stringify({ batchSize: parseInt(batchSize) }), awaits the response and parses JSON, shows toast.success with message `Started ${data.startedTasks} tasks`, then calls a refresh function to reload task data. In finally block, set isExecuting to false. Handle errors with toast.error.

microTasks.change: Implement pause and auto mode toggle handlers
microTasks.description: Create a handlePause function that: sets isPaused to current => !current (toggle), if becoming paused shows toast.info('Execution paused - current tasks will complete'), if becoming unpaused shows toast.info('Execution resumed'). This controls whether new batches can be started automatically. Also create handleAutoModeToggle that toggles autoMode state and shows appropriate toast messages.

microTasks.change: Create execution control panel card structure
microTasks.description: In the JSX return, find the main content area after the project header. Add a new Card component with className 'mb-6'. Inside CardHeader add CardTitle with text 'Execution Control' and a Settings2 icon. The CardContent should contain a flex container with gap-4 and items-center for the control buttons and selectors.

microTasks.change: Add execution control buttons and auto mode toggle
microTasks.description: Inside the execution control CardContent flex container, add: Button with Play icon and text 'Start Batch' with onClick={handleStartBatch} disabled={isExecuting || isPaused}, Button variant='outline' with Pause/Play icon (conditional) and text 'Pause'/'Resume' with onClick={handlePause}, div with flex items-center gap-2 containing Switch checked={autoMode} onCheckedChange={handleAutoModeToggle} and Label text 'Auto Mode' with SkipForward icon.

microTasks.change: Add batch size selector dropdown
microTasks.description: After the auto mode toggle in the same flex container, add a div with flex items-center gap-2 containing: Label with text 'Batch Size:', Select component with value={batchSize} onValueChange={setBatchSize}. Inside Select add SelectTrigger with className w-20 containing SelectValue, then SelectContent with SelectItem components for values '1', '3', '5', '10' with corresponding display text '1 task', '3 tasks', '5 tasks', '10 tasks'.

tasks.originalDescription: Live monitoring in the project detail:

microTasks.change: Create API endpoint for project task statistics
microTasks.description: Create a new API route file. Import NextRequest, NextResponse from 'next/server' and prisma from '@/lib/prisma'. Export async GET function with request and context params. Extract project id from params. Query prisma.task.groupBy with by: ['status'], where: { projectId: id }, _count: { status: true }. This returns counts per status. Also query for tasks completed today using where status 'completed' AND completedAt gte today's midnight.

microTasks.change: Transform and return task statistics
microTasks.description: After the groupBy query, transform results into a stats object: const stats = { planned: 0, in_progress: 0, completed: 0, failed: 0, completedToday: 0 }. Loop through groupBy results and populate stats[item.status] = item._count.status. Set stats.completedToday from the second query count. Return NextResponse.json(stats) with cache headers 'no-store' to ensure fresh data.

microTasks.change: Create API endpoint for currently executing tasks
microTasks.description: Create another API route for executing tasks detail. Export async GET function. Query prisma.task.findMany with where: { projectId: id, status: 'in_progress' }, select: { id: true, name: true, department: true, startedAt: true }. Return NextResponse.json with tasks array. This endpoint provides real-time info about currently executing tasks for the monitoring panel.

microTasks.change: Add state for monitoring statistics and executing tasks
microTasks.description: Add new state variables for monitoring: const [stats, setStats] = useState({ planned: 0, in_progress: 0, completed: 0, failed: 0, completedToday: 0 }), const [executingTasks, setExecutingTasks] = useState<Array<{id: string, name: string, department: string, startedAt: string}>>([]). These will hold the live monitoring data fetched from the new API endpoints.

microTasks.change: Implement polling for real-time monitoring data
microTasks.description: Create a useEffect hook for polling monitoring data. Inside, create an async function fetchMonitoringData that: fetches `/api/projects/${id}/stats` and `/api/projects/${id}/executing` using Promise.all, parses both responses as JSON, calls setStats and setExecutingTasks with results. Call fetchMonitoringData immediately. Set up const interval = setInterval(fetchMonitoringData, 5000) for 5-second polling. Return cleanup function clearing the interval.

microTasks.change: Create live monitoring stats grid
microTasks.description: Below the Execution Control card, add a new Card with className 'mb-6'. CardHeader with CardTitle 'Live Monitoring' and Activity icon. CardContent containing a grid with grid-cols-4 gap-4. Add four stat boxes each as a div with p-4 bg-muted rounded-lg text-center. First: 'In Queue' with stats.planned, yellow color. Second: 'Executing' with stats.in_progress, blue color with pulse animation. Third: 'Completed Today' with stats.completedToday, green. Fourth: 'Failed' with stats.failed, red with attention styling.

microTasks.change: Display list of currently executing tasks with progress indicators
microTasks.description: Below the stats grid within the same Card, add a div with mt-4 for executing tasks list. Add h4 'Currently Executing:' with text-sm font-medium. Map over executingTasks array: for each task render a div with flex items-center gap-3 p-2 bg-background rounded border. Include department icon based on task.department, task.name in font-medium, elapsed time calculated from task.startedAt using date-fns formatDistanceToNow, and a Loader2 icon with animate-spin class as progress indicator.

microTasks.change: Create department icon and color utility functions
microTasks.description: Create a new utility file. Export a function getDepartmentIcon that takes department: string as parameter. Use a switch statement to return appropriate Lucide icons: 'frontend' returns Layout icon, 'backend' returns Server icon, 'database' returns Database icon, 'devops' returns Cloud icon, 'testing' returns TestTube icon, default returns Code icon. Also export getDepartmentColor function returning tailwind color classes for each department.

Query prisma.activityLog.findMany with where: { projectId: id }, orderBy: { createdAt: 'desc' }, take: 50 to get last 50 activities. Select fields: id, type, message, taskId, createdAt. messages.summary: Build the continuous quality monitoring system.
messages.originalContent:
Build the continuous quality monitoring system.

Project: /root/genplatform. Site: https://app.gen3.ai.
DESIGN PROTECTION: Do NOT modify sidebar.tsx, navbar.tsx, layout.tsx, globals.css.

Task 1: Create src/app/api/projects/[id]/review/route.ts:
- POST triggers code review for the project
- Sends to OpenClaw: "🔍 CODE REVIEW for {projectName} at {projectPath}
  Review the latest changes. Check for:
  1. Code quality and best practices
  2. Security vulnerabilities
  3. Performance issues
  4. Missing error handling
  5. TypeScript type errors
  6. Unused imports and dead code
  
  Report findings as JSON: {issues: [{file, line, severity, description, suggestion}], score: 1-10}"
- Returns {reviewId, status: "reviewing"}

Task 2: Create src/app/api/projects/[id]/security-scan/route.ts:
- POST triggers security scan
- Sends to OpenClaw: "🛡️ SECURITY SCAN for {projectName}
  Check all files for:
  1. Exposed API keys or secrets
  2. SQL injection risks
  3. XSS vulnerabilities

messages.summary: Complete the Chat-Telegram integration so conversations flow both ways.
messages.originalContent:
Complete the Chat-Telegram integration so conversations flow both ways.

Project: /root/genplatform. Site: https://app.gen3.ai.
DESIGN PROTECTION: Do NOT modify sidebar.tsx, navbar.tsx, layout.tsx, globals.css.

Task 1: Create a Telegram webhook receiver in Bridge API:
In /root/genplatform-api, create/update routes/webhook.js:
- POST /api/webhook/telegram receives Telegram updates
- Parses message: from user, text, chat_id
- Stores message in a simple JSON file: /root/genplatform-api/data/messages.json
- Each message: {id, from, text, timestamp, projectId (extracted from context prefix)}
- Returns 200 always (Telegram requires this)

Task 2: Register the route in Bridge API server.js. Restart bridge-api.

Task 3: Set up Telegram webhook:
- The webhook URL should be: https://app.gen3.ai/api/webhook/telegram
- Create src/app/api/webhook/telegram/route.ts in Next.js:
  * Receives POST from Telegram
  * Forwards to Bridge API: POST http://localhost:3001/api/webhook/telegram
  * Returns 200
- Register webhook: curl "https://api.telegram.org/bot{TOKEN}/setWebhook" -d '{"url":"https://app.gen3.ai/api/webhook/telegram"}'

Task 4: Create src/app/api/chat/messages/route.ts:
- GET /api/chat/messages?projectId=X
- Reads from Bridge API: GET http://localhost:3001/api/messages?projectId=X
- Returns messages array for the chat UI

Task 5: In the Chat page, poll for new messages:
- Every 3 seconds: fetch /api/chat/messages?projectId=currentProject
- Add new messages to the chat (messages from OpenClaw appear as AI messages)
- This creates a real-time conversation flow:
  User sends message in chat → goes to Telegram → OpenClaw responds → webhook receives → appears in chat

Task 6: Project context in messages:
- When user sends from project "GenPlatform.ai", message prefix: "[Project: GenPlatform.ai | Path: /root/genplatform]"
- When OpenClaw responds, if response contains project context, extract it and tag the message to that project
- Messages without project context go to "General" channel

After ALL: npm run build && pm2 restart genplatform-app && pm2 restart bridge-api && git add -A && git commit -m "Chat-Telegram: full bidirectional integration" && git push

🔗 Preview: https://app.gen3.ai/dashboard/chat
Do all 6 tasks NOW.

tasks.originalDescription: Create a Telegram webhook receiver in Bridge API:

microTasks.change: Initialize messages.json storage file
microTasks.description: Create the data directory if it doesn't exist and initialize an empty messages JSON file. The file should contain an empty array [] to start. This file will store all incoming Telegram messages with their metadata. Use mkdir -p /root/genplatform-api/data && echo '[]' > /root/genplatform-api/data/messages.json to create this file structure.

microTasks.change: Create webhook.js with file helpers
microTasks.description: Create a new Express router file for handling Telegram webhooks. Import express and fs/path modules at the top. Create router = express.Router(). Define const MESSAGES_FILE = path.join(__dirname, '../data/messages.json'). Create helper function readMessages() that reads and parses the JSON file, returning empty array on error. Create helper function writeMessages(messages) that writes the array back to the file with JSON.stringify(messages, null, 2).

microTasks.change: Add POST /telegram endpoint with message extraction
microTasks.description: Add POST handler router.post('/telegram', (req, res) => {...}). Inside, extract the Telegram update object from req.body. Check if req.body.message exists (ignore other update types like edited_message). Extract: const { message_id, from, chat, text, date } = req.body.message. If text is undefined or empty, return res.sendStatus(200) early. Extract from.id, from.username, from.first_name for sender info.

microTasks.change: Parse project context and create message object
microTasks.description: Continue the POST handler: Parse projectId from message text using regex. If text starts with '[Project:', extract project name: const projectMatch = text.match(/\[Project:\s*([^\|]+)/). Set projectId = projectMatch ? projectMatch[1].trim() : 'general'. Create message object: { id: message_id, chatId: chat.id, from: { id: from.id, username: from.username || 'unknown', name: from.first_name || 'User' }, text: text, timestamp: new Date(date * 1000).toISOString(), projectId: projectId, source: 'telegram' }.

microTasks.change: Save message and return 200 response
microTasks.description: Complete the POST handler: Call const messages = readMessages(). Push the new message object to the array: messages.push(newMessage). Call writeMessages(messages) to persist. Log the received message: console.log('[Webhook] Telegram message received:', { from: from.username, text: text.substring(0, 50), projectId }). Always return res.sendStatus(200) at the end - Telegram requires 200 response to confirm webhook receipt. Export the router: module.exports = router.

microTasks.change: Add GET /messages endpoint with filtering
microTasks.description: Add GET endpoint for retrieving messages: router.get('/messages', (req, res) => {...}). Extract projectId from query: const { projectId } = req.query. Read all messages: const messages = readMessages(). If projectId is provided, filter: const filtered = projectId ? messages.filter(m => m.projectId === projectId || m.projectId === 'general') : messages. Sort by timestamp descending: filtered.sort((a,b) => new Date(b.timestamp) - new Date(a.timestamp)). Return res.json({ messages: filtered.slice(0, 100) }) to limit to last 100 messages.

tasks.originalDescription: Register the route in Bridge API server.js. Restart bridge-api.

microTasks.change: Import webhook routes
microTasks.description: At the top of server.js with other require statements, add: const webhookRoutes = require('./routes/webhook'). This imports the newly created webhook router. Place this import near other route imports if they exist, or after the express and cors imports if this is the first custom route file being added to the project.

microTasks.change: Ensure JSON body parsing middleware
microTasks.description: In the middleware section of server.js, ensure body parsing is configured for JSON: app.use(express.json()). This is critical because Telegram sends webhook updates as JSON POST bodies. If this line already exists, verify it comes before route registration. Also ensure app.use(express.urlencoded({ extended: true })) is present for form data support.

microTasks.change: Mount webhook routes at /api/webhook
microTasks.description: Register the webhook routes with the Express app. Add the line: app.use('/api/webhook', webhookRoutes). This mounts the webhook router at /api/webhook, so the Telegram endpoint becomes /api/webhook/telegram. Also add: app.use('/api', webhookRoutes) to expose /api/messages directly. Place these after other middleware but before any error handlers or the app.listen() call.

microTasks.change: Add webhook request logging
microTasks.description: Add logging middleware specifically for webhook requests to help debug Telegram integration. Before the route registration, add: app.use('/api/webhook', (req, res, next) => { console.log(`[${new Date().toISOString()}] Webhook ${req.method} ${req.path}`, req.body ? JSON.stringify(req.body).substring(0, 200) : ''); next(); }). This logs all webhook requests with truncated body for debugging.

microTasks.change: Verify start script and restart pm2
microTasks.description: Verify the package.json has a start script configured. If not present, add to scripts section: "start": "node server.js". Also ensure "main": "server.js" is set. This ensures pm2 can properly restart the service. After making changes, run: cd /root/genplatform-api && pm2 restart bridge-api --update-env. If bridge-api doesn't exist in pm2, run: pm2 start server.js --name bridge-api.

tasks.originalDescription: Set up Telegram webhook:

microTasks.change: Create telegram webhook route file with imports
microTasks.description: Create the directory structure and new route file. Start with imports: import { NextRequest, NextResponse } from 'next/server'. Define the Bridge API URL constant: const BRIDGE_API_URL = process.env.BRIDGE_API_URL || 'http://localhost:3001'. This file will act as a proxy, receiving Telegram updates and forwarding them to the Bridge API for processing and storage.

microTasks.change: Add POST handler with body parsing
microTasks.description: Implement the POST handler: export async function POST(request: NextRequest) { try { const body = await request.json(); console.log('[Telegram Webhook] Received update:', JSON.stringify(body).substring(0, 200)); }. This extracts the Telegram update payload which contains message, from, chat objects. The logging helps debug webhook delivery issues during development.

microTasks.change: Forward webhook to Bridge API
microTasks.description: Continue the POST handler by forwarding to Bridge API: const response = await fetch(`${BRIDGE_API_URL}/api/webhook/telegram`, { method: 'POST', headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }, body: JSON.stringify(body) }). Check response: if (!response.ok) { console.error('[Telegram Webhook] Bridge API error:', response.status); }. This proxies the exact Telegram payload to the Bridge API for processing.

microTasks.change: Return 200 response to Telegram
microTasks.description: Complete the POST handler with response: Always return 200 to Telegram regardless of internal processing status. In the try block after the fetch, add: return NextResponse.json({ ok: true }, { status: 200 }). Add catch block: } catch (error) { console.error('[Telegram Webhook] Error:', error); return NextResponse.json({ ok: true }, { status: 200 }); }. Telegram will retry webhooks that don't return 200, causing duplicate messages.

microTasks.change: Add GET handler for verification
microTasks.description: Add a GET handler for webhook verification: export async function GET() { return NextResponse.json({ status: 'Telegram webhook endpoint active', timestamp: new Date().toISOString() }); }. This allows manual verification that the endpoint is accessible by visiting https://app.gen3.ai/api/webhook/telegram in a browser. Also useful for health checks.

microTasks.change: Create webhook registration script
microTasks.description: Create a setup script for registering the webhook with Telegram. Content: #!/bin/bash followed by TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN="${TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN:-your_token_here}" and WEBHOOK_URL="https://app.gen3.ai/api/webhook/telegram". Add the curl command: curl -X POST "https://api.telegram.org/bot${TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN}/setWebhook" -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d "{\"url\": \"${WEBHOOK_URL}\", \"allowed_updates\": [\"message\"]}" followed by echo to print the response. Make executable with chmod +x.

tasks.originalDescription: Create src/app/api/chat/messages/route.ts:

microTasks.change: Create messages route with imports
microTasks.description: Create the messages API route file. Add imports: import { NextRequest, NextResponse } from 'next/server'. Define BRIDGE_API_URL constant: const BRIDGE_API_URL = process.env.BRIDGE_API_URL || 'http://localhost:3001'. This endpoint will be called by the chat UI to fetch messages for a specific project, enabling the real-time conversation display.

microTasks.change: Add GET handler with query param extraction
microTasks.description: Implement GET handler: export async function GET(request: NextRequest) { const searchParams = request.nextUrl.searchParams; const projectId = searchParams.get('projectId') || 'general'; const limit = searchParams.get('limit') || '50'; }. Extract query parameters for filtering messages by project and limiting results. The projectId defaults to 'general' for messages without specific project context.

microTasks.change: Fetch messages from Bridge API
microTasks.description: Continue GET handler with Bridge API fetch: try { const response = await fetch(`${BRIDGE_API_URL}/api/messages?projectId=${encodeURIComponent(projectId)}&limit=${limit}`, { method: 'GET', headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }, cache: 'no-store' }). The cache: 'no-store' is critical to prevent Next.js from caching responses, ensuring fresh messages on each poll.

microTasks.change: Handle response and return messages
microTasks.description: Handle the Bridge API response: if (!response.ok) { console.error('[Messages API] Bridge error:', response.status); return NextResponse.json({ messages: [], error: 'Failed to fetch messages' }, { status: 200 }); }. Parse successful response: const data = await response.json(); return NextResponse.json({ messages: data.messages || [], projectId, timestamp: new Date().toISOString() }). Always return 200 with empty array on error to prevent UI crashes.

microTasks.change: Add catch block for error handling
microTasks.description: Add error handling catch block: } catch (error) { console.error('[Messages API] Error:', error instanceof Error ? error.message : 'Unknown error'); return NextResponse.json({ messages: [], error: 'Internal server error', projectId }, { status: 200 }); }. Log detailed errors server-side while returning safe response to client. This prevents the chat UI from breaking when Bridge API is unavailable.

microTasks.change: Add POST handler for sending messages
microTasks.description: Add POST handler for sending messages from chat to be stored: export async function POST(request: NextRequest) { const body = await request.json(); const { text, projectId, userId } = body; const message = { id: Date.now().toString(), from: { id: userId || 'web-user', username: 'User', name: 'Web User' }, text, timestamp: new Date().toISOString(), projectId: projectId || 'general', source: 'web' }; }. This allows the chat UI to persist outgoing messages.

tasks.originalDescription: In the Chat page, poll for new messages:

microTasks.change: Add state for Telegram messages
microTasks.description: Add state for storing messages from Telegram. Near other useState declarations, add: const [telegramMessages, setTelegramMessages] = useState<Array<{id: string, from: {name: string, username: string}, text: string, timestamp: string, source: string}>>([]). Also add: const [lastMessageId, setLastMessageId] = useState<string | null>(null). This tracks messages received from the webhook to prevent duplicates.

microTasks.change: Create fetchMessages function
microTasks.description: Create a fetchMessages function inside the component: const fetchMessages = useCallback(async () => { if (!currentProject?.id) return; try { const response = await fetch(`/api/chat/messages?projectId=${encodeURIComponent(currentProject.id)}`); const data = await response.json(); if (data.messages && data.messages.length > 0) { const newMessages = data.messages.filter((m: any) => m.source === 'telegram'); setTelegramMessages(prev => { const existingIds = new Set(prev.map(p => p.id)); return [...prev, ...newMessages.filter((m: any) => !existingIds.has(m.id))]; }); } } catch (error) { console.error('Failed to fetch messages:', error); } }, [currentProject?.id]);

microTasks.change: Add useEffect for 3-second polling
microTasks.description: Set up polling interval with useEffect: useEffect(() => { fetchMessages(); const intervalId = setInterval(fetchMessages, 3000); return () => clearInterval(intervalId); }, [fetchMessages]). This polls every 3 seconds for new messages. The cleanup function clears the interval when component unmounts or dependencies change to prevent memory leaks.

microTasks.change: Render Telegram messages in chat UI
microTasks.description:
Integrate Telegram messages into the chat display. Find the messages mapping section in the JSX. Before the existing messages.map(), add: {telegramMessages.map((msg) => (
@{msg.from.username} via Telegram

{msg.text}

{new Date(msg.timestamp).toLocaleTimeString()}
))}

microTasks.change: Add live sync status indicator
microTasks.description:
Add visual indicator for real-time status. Near the chat header, add a connection status indicator:
Live sync with Telegram
. This gives users feedback that the chat is actively polling for new messages from the Telegram integration.

microTasks.change: Merge and sort all message sources
microTasks.description: Combine and sort all messages for display. Create a merged messages array: const allMessages = useMemo(() => { const combined = [...messages.map(m => ({...m, source: 'web'})), ...telegramMessages]; return combined.sort((a, b) => new Date(a.timestamp).getTime() - new Date(b.timestamp).getTime()); }, [messages, telegramMessages]). Update the JSX to use allMessages.map() instead of separate message arrays. Style messages differently based on m.source === 'telegram'.

tasks.originalDescription: Project context in messages:

microTasks.change: Add project context prefix to outgoing messages
microTasks.description: Update the message sending function to include project context prefix. Find the handleSendMessage or equivalent function. Modify the message text before sending: const projectContext = currentProject ? `[Project: ${currentProject.name} | Path: ${currentProject.path || '/root/' + currentProject.name.toLowerCase().replace(/\s+/g, '')}]\n` : ''; const messageWithContext = projectContext + inputText;. Send messageWithContext instead of raw inputText to the API.

microTasks.change: Create message parser utility
microTasks.description: Create a utility file for parsing project context from messages. Export function parseProjectContext(text: string): { projectName: string | null, projectPath: string | null, cleanText: string }. Use regex: const match = text.match(/\[Project:\s*([^\|]+)\|\s*Path:\s*([^\]]+)\]/). Return { projectName: match?.[1]?.trim() || null, projectPath: match?.[2]?.trim() || null, cleanText: text.replace(/\[Project:[^\]]+\]\n?/, '').trim() }.

microTasks.change: Import and apply message parser
microTasks.description: Import and use the message parser: import { parseProjectContext } from '@/lib/utils/message-parser'. In the fetchMessages function, when processing incoming Telegram messages, parse each one: const parsed = parseProjectContext(msg.text); const processedMsg = { ...msg, text: parsed.cleanText, projectName: parsed.projectName, projectPath: parsed.projectPath }. This extracts context and cleans the display text.

microTasks.change: Filter messages by project context
microTasks.description: Filter messages by current project context. In the fetchMessages function, after parsing messages, filter: const relevantMessages = processedMessages.filter(m => !m.projectName || m.projectName === currentProject?.name || m.projectName === 'general'). This ensures users only see messages relevant to their current project context, with 'general' messages appearing in all channels.

microTasks.change: Display project badge on contextualized messages
microTasks.description: Add project badge to messages with context. In the message rendering JSX, for messages that have projectName set, add a badge: {msg.projectName && msg.projectName !== 'general' && ({msg.projectName})}. Place this badge before the message text content.

microTasks.change: Add General channel and message counts
